expectation_variance.question:

\[ \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ \hline P(X=x) & \frac{1}{8} & \frac{3}{8} & \frac{3}{8} & \frac{1}{8} \end{array} \]

solution.step: The mean and variance are \( E[X] = \tfrac{3}{2} \) and \( \operatorname{Var}(X) = \tfrac{3}{4} \).
The probability mass function is:
Expected value \( E[X] = \sum_i x_i p_i \):

expectation_variance.question:

\[ \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline \text{frequency} & 2 & 3 & 3 & 2 \end{array} \]

solution.step: The sample has mean \( \bar x = \tfrac{5}{2} \) and variance \( s^2 = \tfrac{21}{20} \).
Record the values with their frequencies (total \( N = 10 \)):
Mean \( \bar x = \frac{1}{N}\sum f_i x_i \):
Variance \( s^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum f_i x_i^2 - \bar x^2 \):

expectation_variance.conceptual_note: Weighting each value by its frequency is the same as computing the mean and variance of the empirical distribution.

\[ \begin{array}{c|ccccc} x & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ \hline \text{frequency} & 2 & 2 & 4 & 1 & 1 \end{array} \]

solution.step: The sample has mean \( \bar x = \tfrac{27}{10} \) and variance \( s^2 = \tfrac{141}{100} \).
Record the values with their frequencies (total \( N = 10 \)):
Mean \( \bar x = \frac{1}{N}\sum f_i x_i \):
Variance \( s^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum f_i x_i^2 - \bar x^2 \):

expectation_variance.conceptual_note: Weighting each value by its frequency is the same as computing the mean and variance of the empirical distribution.

pca.question: A dataset has the covariance matrix \( C = \begin{pmatrix} 8 & 3 \\ 3 & 8 \end{pmatrix} \). Find the principal component directions, the variance along each, and the fraction of total variance explained by the first principal component.

solution.step: The principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. The variances along them are \( \lambda_1 = 11 \) and \( \lambda_2 = 5 \); the first principal component points along \( \vec v_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \).
Form the characteristic polynomial \( \det(C - \lambda I) = 0 \):
Solve by the quadratic formula (discriminant \( 16^2 - 4\cdot55 = 36 \), a perfect square):
Largest eigenvalue \( \lambda_1 = 11 \) gives the first principal direction (solve \( (C-\lambda_1 I)\vec v = 0 \)):
The second principal direction is orthogonal to the first:
Fraction of total variance captured by the first principal component:

pca.conceptual_note: Principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix and the eigenvalues are the variances along them; a unit direction would divide \( \vec v_1 \) by its length, but the direction is what matters.

solution.step: \( \dfrac{\partial\,\mathrm{tr}(AX)}{\partial X} = A^{\top} \) = \( \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \).

matrix_derivative.question: Find \( \dfrac{\partial(\vec a^{\top}X\vec b)}{\partial X} \) for \( \vec a = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} \) and \( \vec b = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} \).

solution.step: \( \dfrac{\partial(\vec a^{\top}X\vec b)}{\partial X} = \vec a\,\vec b^{\top} \) = \( \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 6 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \).
Because \( \vec a^{\top}X\vec b = \sum_{i,j} a_i X_{ij} b_j \), the derivative in \( X_{ij} \) is \( a_i b_j \):
That is the outer product \( \vec a\,\vec b^{\top} \):

matrix_derivative.conceptual_note: A bilinear form in \( X \) has derivative equal to the outer product of its two vectors.
